caveats: a: OEWS publishes the useful occupation mix at NAICS 238300 rather than 238350, and the May 2023 vintage predates some current AI use.
a: Task exposure assignments are judgmental and do not measure realized labor substitution.

a: The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts at ancestor 23835, is rated LOW, and is harmonized to the IPS basis; those issues can materially affect the labor opportunity even if this share is sound.
rho: RICS is global and weighted toward built-environment professionals; AGC respondents are broader and often larger than finish-carpentry targets.
rho: Investment intentions and self-reported use are not verified operational labor savings.
rho: Implementation applies only to the exposed share in a; it does not imply automation of onsite craft work.
e: No source measures lens eligibility for NAICS 238350 or for the modeled EBITDA band.
e: The Census definition includes new work, alterations, maintenance, repairs, ship joinery, and several installation niches, so customer recurrence and owner dependence vary.
e: The frozen firm count n=167 is an ESTIMATE margin-bridged from size-class counts and receipts using a broad engineering/construction margin; eligibility does not repair that uncertainty.
s5: The strongest timing evidence was collected in 2020 and reflects pandemic-era expectations.
s5: Construction-wide surveys mix much larger and more transferable contractors with small owner-led shops.
s5: BizBuySell transactions skew well below the target $1-10M EBITDA band and have no industry population denominator.
q: No representative source reports contract-type shares or post-productivity repricing for NAICS 238350.
q: The result is sensitive to whether savings improve scarce-capacity throughput or trigger lower bids; this range addresses price capture only, not demand volume.
q: Federal construction and one Seattle residential contractor are imperfect endpoints for the national target population.
d5: The BLS occupation is broader than finish carpentry and spans a ten-year rather than five-year horizon.
d5: Census construction spending is nominal and highly aggregated, so it is used only as a cyclical boundary signal.
d5: Interest rates, housing starts, remodeling cycles, tariffs, and factory construction can move the five-year endpoint materially.
o: O*NET covers all carpenters rather than finish-carpentry contractors and reports current, not year-five, automation.
o: Prefabrication can reduce field hours without eliminating accountable installation, so its effect on quantity and operator requirement is difficult to separate.
o: Licensing and permit responsibility vary by state and project, and are not directly measured here.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily project-based subcontract or direct-owner revenue, commonly bid as a fixed price for a defined scope, with some time-and-materials work for uncertain conditions. Recurrence comes from repeat builder, general-contractor, remodeler, property-manager, and institutional relationships rather than subscription contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive installation crews, shells, non-control financing vehicles, shop-based cabinet manufacturing, framing, tile or stone countertop installation, retail product sales without installation, and owner-operator or handyman work supported only by isolated one-off consumer jobs rather than a transferable repeat-customer operation.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market contractors performing onsite finish carpentry, including trim and molding, millwork, built-in cabinets, paneling, stairs, and prefabricated residential door, window, garage-door, cabinet, and countertop installation, where work is repeatedly outsourced by homebuilders, general contractors, remodelers, property operators, or recurring institutional customers.

a.bridge: The source population is the broader 4-digit building-finishing industry and the carpenter occupation, not NAICS 238350 firms in the target EBITDA band. I converted OEWS major-group employment shares and wages into approximate payroll weights, then assigned higher exposure to office, business, sales, estimating, recordkeeping, and management work and low exposure to physical installation work, using O*NET task content and observed construction AI use cases as anchors. The broader 238300 mix may overstate back-office scale relative to small finish-carpentry firms, while omission of unpaid owner administrative time may understate exposure.
caveats: OEWS publishes the useful occupation mix at NAICS 238300 rather than 238350, and the May 2023 vintage predates some current AI use.
Task exposure assignments are judgmental and do not measure realized labor substitution.

The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts at ancestor 23835, is rated LOW, and is harmonized to the IPS basis; those issues can materially affect the labor opportunity even if this share is sound.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task-time study of NAICS 238350 firms, separating owner time, estimators, project managers, office staff, and field carpenters and recording current AI/automation use, would materially move this range.
a.rationale: In May 2023, 70.96% of employment in building-finishing contractors was in construction occupations, versus 8.59% office support, 5.32% management, 4.63% business and financial operations, and 2.94% sales. The dominant carpenter tasks are measuring, cutting, fastening, alignment, inspection, and onsite installation, but records, crew scheduling, specifications, material ordering, and cost estimates are also explicit tasks. Houzz reports construction AI use concentrated in administrative work, sales and marketing, planning and design, and project/client management. A wage-weighted task mapping therefore places current not-yet-automated exposure near one-fifth of compensation rather than near the physical-work majority.

d5.bridge: BLS projects national carpenter employment, which mixes finish carpentry with framing, forms, and other carpentry and reflects productivity as well as demand; Census measures nominal construction spending, not constant-price finish-carpentry service quantity. I use roughly half of BLS's 4% ten-year employment growth as the five-year center, and use the current spending decline plus residential/nonresidential breadth to widen the range. Employment can understate service quantity if productivity rises and overstate it if labor intensity rises; nominal spending can overstate real quantity during cost inflation.
caveats: The BLS occupation is broader than finish carpentry and spans a ten-year rather than five-year horizon.
Census construction spending is nominal and highly aggregated, so it is used only as a cyclical boundary signal.
Interest rates, housing starts, remodeling cycles, tariffs, and factory construction can move the five-year endpoint materially.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar forecast for installed millwork, cabinets, trim, doors, and windows, split between new construction and repair/remodeling and adjusted for prefabrication, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects carpenter employment to rise from 959,000 in 2024 to 1,002,100 in 2034, a 4% increase, citing population-driven homebuilding and factory/nonresidential construction, while warning that modular and prefabricated components reduce carpenter demand. Census reported May 2026 total construction spending 1.5% below May 2025 and first-five-month spending 2.7% lower, indicating near-term cyclicality. A near-flat to modestly growing five-year real service quantity is therefore more defensible than extrapolating nominal construction dollars.

caveats: No source measures lens eligibility for NAICS 238350 or for the modeled EBITDA band.
The Census definition includes new work, alterations, maintenance, repairs, ship joinery, and several installation niches, so customer recurrence and owner dependence vary.
The frozen firm count n=167 is an ESTIMATE margin-bridged from size-class counts and receipts using a broad engineering/construction margin; eligibility does not repair that uncertainty.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level sample of the 167 modeled LMM firms showing customer mix, repeat-revenue percentage, owner hours, second-layer management, licensing continuity, and revenue outside NAICS 238350 would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS scope is coherent around onsite finish installation, and firms large enough to produce $1-10M normalized EBITDA are more likely than microbusinesses to have repeat builder, general-contractor, remodeler, or property relationships. Eligibility is reduced for highly owner-dependent shops, businesses reliant on isolated homeowner jobs, captive installers, mixed retailers/manufacturers, customer-concentrated subcontractors without transferable relationships, and operations lacking a management layer. The active market for sold construction businesses supports transferability for some firms but does not reveal the eligible denominator.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23835 ($10,422,182,568) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23835 ($42,970,056,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23835. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $255,121 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238350); class <5: 23,694 firms, avg revenue/firm $319,251 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,412 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 4,509 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,490,102 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $118,612 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,187 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,294,495 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $262,242 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,297 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,826,678 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $782,204 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 127 firms, avg revenue/firm $39,524,835 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,146,177 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $92,923,275 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,396,693 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 167

o.bridge: O*NET measures carpenter tasks and reported current automation, while BLS describes employment drivers; neither directly measures the future share of finish-carpentry service quantity requiring an accountable operator. I map onsite measurement, cutting, fastening, alignment, inspection, installation, safety, and customer/project coordination to operator-required quantity, then allow displacement from prefabrication, modular components, DIY, retailer-managed installation, and software-enabled self-service. The broad carpenter occupation may overstate structural tasks but still captures the onsite physical and accountable nature of finish installation.
caveats: O*NET covers all carpenters rather than finish-carpentry contractors and reports current, not year-five, automation.
Prefabrication can reduce field hours without eliminating accountable installation, so its effect on quantity and operator requirement is difficult to separate.
Licensing and permit responsibility vary by state and project, and are not directly measured here.
o.change_evidence: Field data from cabinet, millwork, door/window, and trim projects comparing conventional installation with factory-prefabricated, robotic, retailer-managed, and DIY delivery would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: O*NET's core carpenter tasks are predominantly physical and site-specific, and 57% of respondents described the job as not at all automated, with only 19% calling it moderately automated. Even when software improves design, estimating, and coordination, customers still need a licensed or accountable contractor to verify conditions, fit imperfect existing structures, protect finishes, manage safety, and remedy defects. BLS identifies modular and prefabricated components as a headwind, so the estimate allows meaningful productization without assuming software can deliver the installed outcome alone.

q.bridge: A single finish-carpentry contractor documents primarily fixed-price work, while GAO describes federal construction as primarily competitive fixed-price contracting; these show contract mechanics, not the discounted five-year retained share for the national LMM population. I map fixed-price cost responsibility to high retention during an awarded scope, then reduce for time-and-materials jobs, transparent labor billing, competitive rebids, GC purchasing power, renewal repricing, customer sharing, and imitation. The single premium residential firm may overstate fixed-price prevalence, while federal procurement may overstate price competition and formal rebidding.
caveats: No representative source reports contract-type shares or post-productivity repricing for NAICS 238350.
The result is sensitive to whether savings improve scarce-capacity throughput or trigger lower bids; this range addresses price capture only, not demand volume.
Federal construction and one Seattle residential contractor are imperfect endpoints for the national target population.
q.change_evidence: A contract-level panel from finish-carpentry firms showing fixed-price versus time-and-materials revenue, bid frequency, gross-margin change after workflow deployment, price changes at renewal/rebid, win rates, and customer concentration would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed-price scopes allow a contractor to retain much of a labor saving until the job is rebid; the cited finish-carpentry firm says it primarily quotes fixed prices and adds 15%-20% above estimated cost for uncertainty. GAO says federal construction was primarily competitively awarded at fixed prices with the prime responsible for price and schedule and subcontractors performing 60%-90% of work. Across five years, repeated competitive bids and knowledgeable general contractors should pass through a meaningful share, while service quality, responsiveness, estimating data, and execution reliability preserve part of the benefit.

rho.bridge: The sources measure current adoption, investment plans, and perceived barriers among broad construction and design populations, not the five-year implemented share of exposed labor in LMM finish-carpentry firms. I translate current use and pilots into five-year workflow implementation, giving credit to embedded estimating, documentation, scheduling, invoicing, and communication tools but discounting for small-firm resources, training, integration, accuracy review, and field-office fragmentation. Survey respondents and larger digital firms are likely to overstate implementation for the target population.
caveats: RICS is global and weighted toward built-environment professionals; AGC respondents are broader and often larger than finish-carpentry targets.
Investment intentions and self-reported use are not verified operational labor savings.
Implementation applies only to the exposed share in a; it does not imply automation of onsite craft work.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of 20-100 employee specialty contractors measuring production deployment, hours removed by workflow, error/rework rates, training, and integration costs over at least two years would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Houzz found 32% of construction businesses using AI, rising to 64% among large firms, but 92% of contractor users had no formal AI training. RICS found 45% with no implementation, 34% in pilots, about 15% actively using AI, and 74% with limited or no preparation, while AGC found 44% planned higher AI investment and reported implementation time, employee resistance, and integration as major IT challenges. Five years should permit meaningful deployment of bounded office workflows, but accountable review and fragmented operating data prevent full conversion of exposed tasks.

s5.bridge: FMI/CFMA measures intended ownership transitions among construction executives, SBA measures broad owner intentions and acquisition paths, and BizBuySell records selected small construction businesses that actually sold; none measures five-year control transfers among eligible LMM finish-carpentry firms. I center below the 29%-35% five-year construction intention/exit readings to allow for delays, partial equity sales, failed processes, and nontransferable owner dependence, while retaining upside for internal or family control succession. CFMA's larger, professionally managed respondents may overstate planning and completion, whereas marketplace data underrepresent private internal transfers.
caveats: The strongest timing evidence was collected in 2020 and reflects pandemic-era expectations.
Construction-wide surveys mix much larger and more transferable contractors with small owner-led shops.
BizBuySell transactions skew well below the target $1-10M EBITDA band and have no industry population denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A current NAICS-238350 owner census or broker/quality-of-earnings dataset linking owner age, intended exit year, planned transfer method, signed LOIs, completed control changes, and post-close operating continuity would materially move this probability.
s5.rationale: The 2021 FMI/CFMA survey reported 29% expected to have sold 100% of equity within five years after pandemic disruption and 35% planned to exit in less than five years; more than two-thirds intended an internal transfer to employees or family. SBA reported 64.5% of owners planned eventually to sell, but only 12% of construction owners had acquired their business by purchase. BizBuySell analyzed 3,142 reported construction sales from 2021-2025, demonstrating a functioning market but not a probability denominator. A five-year qualifying-control probability in the high twenties balances intent against execution and transferability risk.

principal_driver: Durable onsite installation demand paired with automatable estimating, documentation, scheduling, and back-office workflows.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and site-specific, while transferability and retained savings depend heavily on owner independence and repeat-customer quality that public data do not measure.
